title: The Cross-Task Tradeoff
concept: Over-weighting one task (like coding) in the dataset mix can degrade the model's performance on other tasks (like creative writing).

exercises.prompt: What usually happens to an AI's skills when you train it mostly on coding and very little on writing?

options.text: One gets better while the other gets worse.
They both stay exactly the same.

exercises.explain: When we focus too much on training one skill, the other skills tend to get worse. This is known as a tradeoff.

cards.title: The Balancing Act
cards.body: Imagine you want to build an AI that is both a master programmer and a brilliant novelist. Sounds easy, right? Just give it tons of code and tons of books! But if you don't balance the mix perfectly, you might end up with an AI that writes buggy code and terrible stories. Let's find out why!

cards.title: What is the Cross-Task Tradeoff?
cards.body: The Cross-Task Tradeoff is what happens when you train an AI on too much of one thing. If you over-feed it one task (like programming code), it gets much better at that task, but its performance on other tasks (like poetry or general chat) starts to drop.

cards.title: The Math-Only Student
cards.body: Imagine a student preparing for exams. If they spend 95% of their time practicing math and only 5% on history, they will ace the math test but fail history. In AI, if we feed the model almost all coding data, it 'forgets' how to write stories or essays.

cards.title: The Over-Trained Coder
cards.body: An AI lab wants to make a coding assistant, so they train a model with 90% Python code. The model becomes an amazing coder! However, when users ask it to write a simple email to a friend, it writes awkward sentences and uses computer jargon because it lost its general writing skills.

cards.title: Does More Data Always Help?
cards.body: You might think that adding more data of any kind always makes an AI smarter overall. In reality, adding too much data for one specific task can actually crowd out and ruin the model's ability to do other tasks. More isn't always better!

cards.title: Visualizing the Tradeoff
cards.body: When we change the mixture of training data, the model's strengths shift. We cannot maximize both tasks fully without finding the right balance.

left.text: Great at writing code, but writes robotic, dry essays.

right.text: Good at coding and writes natural, creative stories.

exercises.prompt: Why does over-weighting coding data hurt an AI's ability to write creative stories?

options.text: Coding data makes the AI run out of storage space.
The AI prioritizes coding patterns and loses its grasp on creative language.
Creative writing requires a paid subscription for the AI.
Coding data contains viruses that erase the AI's memory.

exercises.explain: When one task dominates the training data, the AI shifts its focus too much toward that task, which hurts its performance on other, under-represented tasks.

exercises.prompt: To make a good general AI, developers must carefully balance the ratio of different tasks in the training data.
exercises.explain: This is true. Training an AI requires balancing different types of data so it doesn't get too good at one thing while failing at others.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the rule of training balance.
exercises.sentence: Over-weighting one task in the training mix can _____ the model's performance on other tasks.
exercises.explain: If we push too much data for a single task like coding, we will degrade (or lower) the model's performance on other tasks.

exercises.prompt: We asked three different AI models to write a story about a dragon. Which model is showing signs of the Cross-Task Tradeoff after being fed too much coding data?

options.text: System A: "Once upon a time, a friendly green dragon lived in a dark cave."
System B: "def dragon(): print('Once upon a time')"
System C: "A fire-breathing beast flew over the tall, snowy mountains."

exercises.explain: System B is the AI that shows the classic symptom of the tradeoff: it was fed too much code, so now it tries to solve a creative writing prompt using programming syntax.

exercises.prompt: If you build a new AI model and train it using 99% coding examples and only 1% creative writing examples, what is the most likely outcome?

options.text: It becomes equally genius at all tasks at the same time.
It gets great at the over-represented task but gets worse at other tasks.
It completely forgets all human languages and stops working.
It automatically splits its brain to handle both perfectly.

exercises.explain: When you flood a model's training data with one specific task, it adjusts its internal settings to excel at that task, which directly damages its ability to handle other types of tasks. Balance is essential.
